narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our model predicts a 62% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid background as a white male from an upper-middle-class family, planning to attend full-time, with a first-year school decile of 5—right around the typical student's level—and an LSAT score of 34, which is a bit below the average of 36.7. However, your third-year decile of 1 stands out as much lower than the usual 5.53, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 falls short of the average 3.21, which raised some concerns. The model's decision was driven mostly by your academic performance, with your third-year decile having the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure, followed closely by your first-year decile. Your family income bracket played a notable role next in that direction, then your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and racial background each contributed somewhat toward expecting a fail. Being male nudged things slightly in a positive direction toward passing, as did your full-time status, but these had the least impact overall. Compared to typical students—who often maintain stronger deciles across years, higher GPAs, and LSATs—your profile showed a dip in later performance that the model flagged as the biggest risk. This isn't a judgment on your potential, just a data-driven assessment to help schools set up students for success. To boost your future chances, focus on rebuilding your academic momentum, perhaps by retaking the LSAT to aim above 36.7 or strengthening your study habits to avoid another low decile in advanced coursework. Your full-time commitment is already a plus, so lean into that dedication. With targeted improvements in these areas, you could shift the prediction favorably next time—many students turn things around by addressing exactly these gaps. Keep pushing; your upper-middle background gives you resources to prep effectively for the bar.
